class_schedule.topic: Financial Management Fundamentals and Time Value of Money
learning_objectives: Explain the role of financial management in organizations
Calculate present value and future value of single and multiple cash flows
Apply time value of money to project evaluation decisions
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance, Ch. 1-2
Ross, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance, Ch. 5

lecture_notes.introduction: Financial management is about making decisions that maximize value. The time value of money is the foundation: a dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow.
key_concepts: Financial management goal: maximize shareholder/stakeholder value through investment, financing, and operating decisions
Time value of money: money earns returns over time, so future cash flows must be discounted
Present Value: PV = FV / (1 + r)^n where r = discount rate, n = number of periods
Future Value: FV = PV x (1 + r)^n
Annuity calculations: equal payments over multiple periods
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating the PV of a project that generates $50,000/year for 5 years at a 10% discount rate

review.value: Reflects the opportunity cost of capital; money invested in this project could have been invested elsewhere for a return
PV = Payment x [(1 - (1+r)^-n) / r]; commonly used for evaluating projects with equal annual cash flows

class_schedule.topic: Investment Decision Criteria: NPV, IRR, and Payback
learning_objectives: Calculate and interpret Net Present Value (NPV)
Calculate and interpret Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
Compare NPV, IRR, and payback period as project selection tools

lecture_notes.introduction: Organizations must choose between competing investments. NPV, IRR, and payback period are the primary tools for making these decisions.
key_concepts: NPV: sum of all discounted cash flows including initial investment; NPV > 0 means value is created
IRR: discount rate that makes NPV = 0; compare to required rate of return (hurdle rate)
Payback period: time to recover the initial investment; simple but ignores time value and cash flows after payback
NPV vs. IRR conflicts: when they disagree, NPV is the more reliable decision criterion
Profitability Index: PV of future cash flows / initial investment; useful for capital-constrained decisions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating two mutually exclusive projects using NPV, IRR, and payback, and resolving the conflict between them
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet with NPV and IRR functions

review.value: Accept projects with NPV > 0; among mutually exclusive projects, choose the one with the highest NPV
Can give misleading results for mutually exclusive projects of different sizes or with non-conventional cash flows; always verify with NPV

learning_objectives: Read and interpret the three primary financial statements
Calculate key financial ratios: liquidity, profitability, leverage, efficiency
Assess organizational financial health from published data

lecture_notes.introduction: Financial statements tell the story of an organization's financial performance. Project managers need to read them to understand organizational context and project impact.
key_concepts: Income statement: revenue - expenses = net income; measures profitability over a period
Balance sheet: assets = liabilities + equity; snapshot of financial position at a point in time
Cash flow statement: operating, investing, and financing activities; cash is king
Liquidity ratios: current ratio, quick ratio (can the company pay short-term obligations?)
Profitability ratios: net margin, ROE, ROA; leverage: debt-to-equity; efficiency: asset turnover
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing Apple Inc.'s financial statements and calculating key ratios to assess financial health

review.value: A company can be profitable on paper but bankrupt if it cannot pay its bills; cash flow matters more than net income for survival
Ratios are meaningful only in comparison: to the same company over time, to industry averages, or to competitors

learning_objectives: Prepare operating and capital budgets
Apply variance analysis to budget performance
Understand the relationship between budgeting and project cost management

lecture_notes.introduction: Budgets translate plans into financial terms. Variance analysis reveals whether the organization is executing according to plan.
key_concepts: Operating budget: revenue and expense projections for the operating period
Capital budget: planned investments in long-term assets and projects
Budget variance: actual vs. budgeted amounts; favorable (under budget) or unfavorable (over budget)
Flexible budgets: adjusting budgeted amounts for actual activity levels before comparing
Project budgeting: bottom-up estimates aggregated into cost baselines with reserves
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performing variance analysis on a department's quarterly budget and identifying root causes of variances

review.value: Adjusts the budget for actual volume before comparing to actuals; prevents misleading variances due to volume changes
The organizational process for evaluating and approving project investments; connects to portfolio management and NPV analysis

class_schedule.topic: Risk and Return in Financial Decision-Making
learning_objectives: Explain the risk-return trade-off in investment decisions
Apply the weighted average cost of capital (WACC) as a discount rate
Understand how financial risk affects project evaluation

lecture_notes.introduction: Higher returns require accepting higher risk. Financial management quantifies this trade-off to ensure organizations are compensated for the risks they take.
key_concepts: Risk-return trade-off: investors demand higher returns for higher risk
Diversification: combining uncorrelated investments reduces portfolio risk
WACC: weighted average of the cost of equity and cost of debt; the minimum return a project must earn
Risk-adjusted discount rates: higher risk projects require higher hurdle rates
Sensitivity analysis: testing how changes in key assumptions affect project NPV
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating WACC for a company and using it to evaluate a project investment with sensitivity analysis on three key variables

review.value: The blended cost of all capital sources; if a project earns less than WACC, it destroys value; if more, it creates value
Change one variable at a time to see which assumptions have the greatest impact on NPV; focuses risk management on the most sensitive variables

learning_objectives: Build a business case with financial justification for a project proposal
Apply break-even analysis and ROI calculations
Communicate financial information to non-financial stakeholders
readings: Harvard Business Review, Guide to Finance for Managers (selected chapters)

lecture_notes.introduction: Project managers who speak the language of finance get their projects approved. The ability to build a compelling financial case is a career-differentiating skill.
key_concepts: Business case structure: problem, solution, costs, benefits, risks, alternatives, recommendation
Break-even analysis: the point where total revenue equals total cost; the minimum for viability
ROI calculation: (Net benefit / Cost) x 100%; simple but widely understood metric
Total cost of ownership (TCO): all costs over the life of an investment, not just the initial purchase
Financial storytelling: translating numbers into a narrative that drives decision-making
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a business case for a technology investment including NPV, ROI, break-even, and TCO analysis

review.value: A $50K software license with $30K/year maintenance costs $200K over 5 years; TCO reveals the true cost beyond the purchase price
Don't just present numbers; explain what they mean: 'This $500K investment returns $1.2M in 3 years, paying for itself in 18 months'

class_schedule.topic: Working Capital and Cash Flow Management
learning_objectives: Explain the components of working capital and their management
Prepare and analyze cash flow projections
Understand how project cash flows affect organizational liquidity

lecture_notes.introduction: Cash flow management keeps the organization alive. Even profitable projects can cause cash crises if their cash flow timing is not managed.
key_concepts: Working capital: current assets minus current liabilities; the cash needed to operate day-to-day
Cash conversion cycle: days inventory + days receivable - days payable; how long cash is tied up
Cash flow projection: forecasting cash inflows and outflows over time
Project cash flow impact: large projects consume cash before generating returns
Funding strategy: matching project cash needs with available funding mechanisms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a 12-month cash flow projection for a company with a large project investment and identifying the maximum cash need

review.value: Shorter is better; reducing inventory days or receivable days or increasing payable days frees up cash
A $1M project with front-loaded costs and back-loaded benefits creates a cash trough that must be funded

quizzes.topic: Time Value of Money and Investment Criteria

questions.question: Project X requires a $200,000 investment and generates cash flows of $60,000/year for 5 years. The company's required rate of return is 12%. Calculate the NPV and determine whether the project should be accepted.
Explain why NPV is considered superior to payback period for project evaluation, and describe one situation where payback period might still be useful.
questions.answer: NPV is superior because it: (1) accounts for the time value of money (payback does not), (2) considers ALL cash flows over the project life (payback ignores cash flows after the payback point), (3) provides a dollar value of wealth created (payback only measures time to recover investment), (4) can correctly compare projects of different sizes and durations. However, payback period is still useful when: liquidity is a critical concern and the organization needs to recover its cash quickly (e.g., a startup with limited capital), or as a quick screening tool to eliminate projects that take too long to pay back before doing detailed NPV analysis.

rubric.key: At least 3 NPV advantages over payback
Identifies a legitimate use for payback

questions.question: A company has total assets of $5M, total liabilities of $3M, revenue of $8M, net income of $600K, and current assets of $2M with current liabilities of $1.5M. Calculate: (a) debt-to-equity ratio, (b) net profit margin, (c) ROA, and (d) current ratio.
questions.answer: Equity = Assets - Liabilities = $5M - $3M = $2M. (a) Debt-to-equity = $3M / $2M = 1.5. (b) Net profit margin = $600K / $8M = 7.5%. (c) ROA = $600K / $5M = 12%. (d) Current ratio = $2M / $1.5M = 1.33.

rubric.key: Correct net profit margin and ROA

questions.question: A company's WACC is 10%. A proposed project has an expected return of 13% but is in a riskier industry than the company's core business. Should the company use 10% as the discount rate for this project? Why or why not?
questions.answer: No, the company should NOT use its overall WACC of 10% for this project. WACC reflects the average risk of the company's existing portfolio of investments. A project in a riskier industry faces higher uncertainty, so it should be evaluated at a higher risk-adjusted discount rate (perhaps 14-16%, depending on the industry risk premium). Using 10% would overstate the NPV and make the project look more attractive than it actually is on a risk-adjusted basis. The correct approach is to use a discount rate that reflects the project's specific risk, not the company's average risk. If the project's 13% return is compared to a risk-appropriate hurdle rate of 15%, the project should be rejected even though 13% > 10% WACC.

rubric.key: Correctly says WACC should not be used
Explains that WACC reflects average, not project-specific risk

major_assignments.title: Project Financial Analysis and Business Case
major_assignments.description: Build a comprehensive financial analysis and business case for a project investment, including NPV, IRR, sensitivity analysis, and financial risk assessment.
objectives: Perform NPV and IRR analysis for a project with multi-year cash flows
Conduct sensitivity analysis on key financial assumptions
Build a professional business case document with financial justification
Present the financial case to a simulated investment committee
deliverables: Financial model spreadsheet with NPV, IRR, payback, and ROI calculations
Sensitivity analysis showing NPV impact of 3 key variable changes
Business case document (5-6 pages) with executive summary, financial analysis, risk assessment, and recommendation
5-minute presentation to class simulating an investment committee pitch

criteria: Cash flow projections are realistic and well-documented
NPV, IRR, payback, and ROI correctly calculated
Three key variables identified and justified
Analysis identifies which variables matter most
Financial analysis summarized accessibly for non-financial audience

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on TVM, investment criteria, financial statements, and budgeting

financial_project.description: Project financial analysis and business case

midterm_examination.description: TVM calculations, NPV/IRR, and financial statement analysis

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all financial management topics

class_participation.description: Participation in financial analysis exercises, case studies, and investment discussions

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Financial calculation workshops require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Financial exercises may be done in pairs. The business case is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All calculations must be your own work. Financial models must show formulas, not just answers.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Spreadsheet application with financial functions (PV, NPV, IRR) required. Financial calculator recommended.
